serving.natural_language_portion: One medium lime weighs approximately 67 g and is commonly used whole, juiced, or zested as a flavouring in beverages, salads, and cooked dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Lime, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw lime (Citrus aurantifolia). Sugar and saturated fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09159 (Limes, raw) where HPB did not report disaggregated values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for lime (Citrus aurantifolia) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with other low-GI citrus fruits (lemon GI ~20, orange GI ~40–45) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 25 × 7.06 / 100 ≈ 1.8 (based on 67 g serving with 10.54 g carbohydrate/100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (102 mg/100 g) content
hypertension.guidance: Lime is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content and moderate potassium supply align with DASH dietary principles, and regular inclusion of citrus fruits is associated with modest reductions in systolic blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25), low sugar (~1.69 g/100 g), and 2.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lime is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes; its very low GI and low sugar content produce minimal postprandial glucose excursion, while its soluble fibre and citric acid may further attenuate glycaemic response — consistent with ADA dietary guidance on low-GI fruit inclusion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high vitamin C (29.1 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lime is cardioprotective; its negligible saturated fat and cholesterol content, combined with vitamin C and flavonoids (hesperidin, naringenin), support endothelial function and reduce LDL oxidation, consistent with AHA dietary recommendations for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (29.1 mg/100 g) and anti-inflammatory flavonoids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lime provides meaningful vitamin C — a key antioxidant that reduces oxidative stress in rheumatoid arthritis — and its flavonoids (hesperidin, naringenin) offer additional anti-inflammatory polyphenol activity that may help moderate disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lime (Citrus aurantifolia) is a small, round, green-skinned citrus fruit with pale yellow-green flesh, numerous seeds, and intensely sour juice. It is consumed raw and is never cooked as a standalone food; instead it is juiced, zested, or sliced and used as a flavouring and souring agent across Southeast Asian, South Asian, Latin American, and global cuisines. In Singapore and Malaysia, lime (limau nipis) is ubiquitous as a condiment alongside hawker dishes such as laksa, char kway teow, and grilled meats, and is a core ingredient in beverages such as lime juice and calamansi-lime blends. Nutritionally, lime is a low-calorie fruit (30 kcal/100 g) with a low glycemic index (~25), very low sugar (~1.69 g/100 g), and meaningful dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g). It is an excellent source of vitamin C (29.1 mg/100 g), which supports immune function, collagen synthesis, and antioxidant defence. Its negligible sodium (2 mg/100 g), low saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), and zero cholesterol make it suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The flavonoids hesperidin and naringenin present in lime peel and juice have demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties in peer-reviewed research. Lime is fully plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lime?
Is lime high in vitamin C?
Can I eat lime if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of lime?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match). Sugar, fatty acid fractions, and micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09159 (Limes, raw). GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed citrus GI data (Atkinson et al. 2008). All values are internally consistent and plausible.
notes: Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central; lime is a plant food and inherently contains no cholesterol. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as lime is a plant food containing no vitamin B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 09159; lime contains negligible vitamin D. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 09159. Saturated_fat_g (0.02 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.02 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.06 g), sugar_g (1.69 g), and all micronutrient values not reported by HPB are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09159 (Limes, raw), accessed 2026-04-16. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on citrus fruit GI data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Citrus aurantifolia was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. Local-language names provided: Chinese (青柠 / 青檸), Malay (Limau nipis), Tamil (எலுமிச்சை), Hindi (नींबू) — included to support multilingual retrieval in the Singapore context where lime is a staple hawker condiment.
